text: The video shows a small-sized mass located in the fundus of the stomach, visually confirmed through imaging.
The surgeon performs diagnostic laparoscopy, visually inspecting the liver, peritoneum, and surrounding areas for signs of hepatic, peritoneal, or distant metastasis.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the greater omentum from the transverse colon on the left side, progressing to complete the dissection on the right side.
The surgeon performs dissection along the gastroepiploic blood vessels and station IV lymph nodes, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon dissects along the retroduodenal space, performing skeletonization of station V and station VI lymph nodes located in the suprapyloric and intrapyloric areas.
The surgeon performs a duodenal transection distal to the pylorus and oversews the duodenal stump using two 00 absorbable sutures.
The surgeon performs dissection along the pars flaccida and station III lymph nodes located along the lesser curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the left gastric vessels away from the station seven lymph nodes, ensuring clear separation of these structures.
The surgeon clips and divides the gastroepiploic blood vessels, completing the dissection around the station IV lymph nodes.
The surgeon continues dissection into the retrogastric space, performing skeletonization of station eleven lymph nodes, carefully separating them from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon performs dissection along the right and left crura, skeletonizing station one and station two lymph nodes, with visible separation of these structures from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon dissects the phrenoesophageal fat pad and mobilizes the esophagus into the abdominal cavity, followed by the transection of the esophagus.
The surgeon continues dissection along the greater curvature of the stomach, moving towards the spleen. The posterior and lateral attachments of the spleen are carefully dissected.
The surgeon identifies the splenic hilum, visually distinguishing it from surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon completes the splenectomy and retrieves the excised spleen and associated specimens from the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to perform a Roux-en-Y operation, creating an esophago-jejunostomy by connecting the esophagus to the jejunum.
The surgeon creates a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy between the biliopancreatic limb and the common channel, using surgical instruments to align and connect the two sections of the jejunum.
The surgeon advances the Roux limb into the left upper quadrant, positioning it for the esophago-jejunostomy.
The surgeon completes the esophago-jejunostomy using a 25 mm circular stapler, joining the esophagus to the jejunum with precise stapling technique.
The surgeon transects the candy cane segment and places anchoring sutures on the left and right crura to ensure a tension-free anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a leak test to assess the patency of the esophagojejunostomy anastomosis, visually inspecting for any signs of leakage.
